fullDescription.text: The passport token issued for non-enterprise (public) users does not include an 'exp' claim. JWTs without expiration never become invalid, allowing indefinite access if a token is leaked. An attacker who obtains a passport token can reuse it forever.

shortDescription.text: User-controlled session ID enables session fixation

fullDescription.text: The 'user_id' query parameter is accepted directly from the request and used as the session_id for EndUser lookup or creation. An attacker can choose an arbitrary session_id value and trick a victim into using it (e.g. via a crafted link), resulting in a session fixation attack where the attacker knows the victim's session identifier.

shortDescription.text: Insecure direct object reference via user-controlled session binding

fullDescription.text: When 'user_id' is supplied, the endpoint looks up an existing EndUser by session_id without verifying that the caller owns that session. Any caller who knows or guesses another user's session_id can obtain a valid JWT passport bound to that user's EndUser record, gaining access to their conversation history and data.

shortDescription.text: Exchanged token expiration controlled by untrusted claim

fullDescription.text: In exchange_token_for_existing_web_user, the 'exp' value from the decoded enterprise token is trusted directly and used as the expiration for the newly issued passport. If the original token's 'exp' was set far in the future, the exchanged passport inherits that extended lifetime, bypassing the server's intended ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES policy.

shortDescription.text: Application and site enumeration via differential error responses

fullDescription.text: The endpoint returns different HTTP status codes (404 vs 200) depending on whether the app_code corresponds to an existing, active site. An attacker can brute-force app_code values to enumerate valid applications.

message.text: The JWT passport issued in the public (non-enterprise) flow has no 'exp' claim in its payload. The payload contains only 'iss', 'sub', 'app_id', 'app_code', and 'end_user_id'. Without expiration, a leaked token grants permanent access. The same issue exists in _exchange_for_public_app_token.

The query parameter 'user_id' is used directly as session_id (line 58). An attacker can craft a URL with a known user_id value. When a victim visits this URL, an EndUser record is created (or looked up) with the attacker-chosen session_id. The attacker can then request a passport with the same user_id to obtain a token bound to the victim's session.

When 'user_id' is provided, the endpoint looks up an existing EndUser by session_id (line 58-60) and issues a JWT passport for that EndUser without any ownership verification. Any unauthenticated caller who knows or guesses a valid session_id can obtain a passport token for another user's session.

In exchange_token_for_existing_web_user, the expiration from the incoming enterprise token (exchanged_token_expires_unix) is used directly as the new token's 'exp' (line 134-135). If the enterprise token has an 'exp' far in the future, the exchanged passport inherits that extended lifetime, ignoring ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES. The server should enforce its own expiration policy.

The endpoint returns 404 when app_code does not match a valid site and 200 with a token when it does. This differential response allows an attacker to enumerate valid application codes by brute-forcing the X-App-Code header.
